home.learn.title: Learn
home.learn.description: Understand traffic situations through guided demonstrations and actions.
home.practice.title: Practice
home.practice.description: Work through traffic scenarios with progressively less assistance.
home.simulate.title: Simulate
home.simulate.description: Control predefined traffic freely in the simulation environment.
footer.disclaimer: Training and visualisation software only. Not for real aircraft operations or live traffic control. All airspaces and scenarios are fictional.
learn.title: Learn
learn.subtitle: Understand traffic situations through guided simulation.
concept.plannedNote: This concept group is planned and will arrive in a later update.

curriculum.separation_fundamentals.title: Separation Fundamentals
curriculum.separation_fundamentals.descr: The foundations controllers use to keep aircraft safely apart.
curriculum.traffic_relationships.title: Traffic Relationships
curriculum.traffic_relationships.descrip: Understand how aircraft tracks relate to one another before applying separation.
curriculum.traffic_relationships.overvie: Learn how to recognise same-track, reciprocal-track, crossing-track, and unrelated traffic. Understanding how aircraft tracks relate is essential before applying many forms of separation.
curriculum.traffic_relationships.next_no: You can now move from identifying crossing traffic to managing it.
curriculum.vertical_separation.title: Vertical Separation
curriculum.vertical_separation.descripti: Learn how aircraft are separated by assigned and occupied levels.
curriculum.horizontal_separation.title: Horizontal Separation
curriculum.horizontal_separation.descrip: Explore lateral and longitudinal separation between aircraft.
curriculum.crossing_traffic.title: Crossing Traffic
curriculum.crossing_traffic.description: Understand how two aircraft on converging tracks can develop a conflict and learn one way to resolve it.
lessons.classify.question: What is the traffic relationship?
lessons.tr_understanding_track.title: Understanding Track
lessons.tr_understanding_track.steps.obs: The aircraft is following a route across the airspace. Watch how it moves along the published path.
lessons.tr_understanding_track.steps.tra: Track is the direction of the aircraft's path over the ground. The short vector ahead of the aircraft symbol shows the direction it is actually travelling.
lessons.tr_understanding_track.steps.rou: The route is the planned path between fixes. The track describes the direction the aircraft is actually travelling right now.
lessons.tr_understanding_track.steps.hea: Heading is the direction the aircraft is pointed. Track is the direction it moves over the ground. In this simulation they usually match, but the traffic relationship is always judged from tracks.
lessons.tr_understanding_track.steps.che: Which element represents the aircraft's track?
The motion vector shows the direction of the aircraft's path over the ground — its track. The route line is the planned path, and the label only carries information.
You can now distinguish an aircraft's route, its track, and its heading. Next, compare the tracks of two aircraft.
lessons.tr_same_track.title: Same-Track Traffic
lessons.tr_same_track.steps.observe.text: Both aircraft are travelling along the same route in the same direction.
lessons.tr_same_track.steps.direction.te: Look at the motion vectors: the aircraft share the same general track direction.
lessons.tr_same_track.steps.leader.text: One aircraft is leading and the other is following along the same track.
lessons.tr_same_track.steps.spacing.text: The follower is faster, so the distance between them is reducing. Their relationship remains same track even when the spacing changes.
lessons.tr_same_track.steps.classify.exp: Both aircraft follow the same route in the same direction — one leading, one following. That is same-track traffic, regardless of the distance between them.
lessons.tr_same_track.complete.title: Same track recognised
lessons.tr_same_track.complete.text: You can now recognise aircraft sharing the same track. Next, see what happens when two aircraft use the same path in opposite directions.
lessons.tr_reciprocal_track.title: Reciprocal-Track Traffic
lessons.tr_reciprocal_track.steps.observ: The aircraft are travelling along the same route in opposite directions.
lessons.tr_reciprocal_track.steps.closur: Their track directions are opposite and the distance between them is reducing quickly. This closure is what makes reciprocal traffic demanding.
lessons.tr_reciprocal_track.steps.relati: This is reciprocal-track traffic: the same or substantially the same path, flown in opposite directions.
lessons.tr_reciprocal_track.steps.classi: The aircraft use the same path in opposite directions, closing on each other. That is reciprocal-track traffic.
lessons.tr_reciprocal_track.complete.tex: You can now recognise opposite-direction traffic on the same path. Next, look at tracks that intersect.
lessons.tr_crossing_track.title: Crossing-Track Traffic
The aircraft are approaching from different directions.
lessons.tr_crossing_track.steps.intersec: Follow both routes ahead of the aircraft: their tracks intersect at a common point.
lessons.tr_crossing_track.steps.relation: This is crossing-track traffic. This lesson is about recognising the geometry — managing the crossing comes later.
lessons.tr_crossing_track.steps.classify: The two tracks meet at a common crossing point ahead of both aircraft. That is crossing-track traffic.
lessons.tr_crossing_track.complete.title: Crossing track recognised
lessons.tr_crossing_track.complete.text: You can now recognise intersecting tracks. Finish the journey by classifying a series of situations yourself.
lessons.tr_identify_relationship.title: Identify the Relationship
lessons.tr_identify_relationship.steps.i: You will now see a series of traffic situations. For each one, classify the relationship from the aircraft tracks — not from how close the aircraft appear.
lessons.tr_identify_relationship.steps.e: Both aircraft follow the same route in the same direction: same-track traffic.
The aircraft use the same path in opposite directions, closing on each other: reciprocal-track traffic.
The tracks intersect at a common point ahead of both aircraft: crossing-track traffic.
The tracks lead away from each other and never meet ahead of the aircraft: no same, reciprocal, or crossing relationship.
The aircraft are close to the same fix, but their tracks lead away from each other. Closeness alone does not create a traffic relationship — always judge from the tracks.
account.title: Your account
account.switchToLogin: Already have an account? Sign in
account.guestNote: You can use Learn, Practice, and solo Simulate without an account. Signing in adds saved progress, run history, and your language preference across devices.
